title: CoreDataEnsemble
summary: The central class of Ensembles, representing a set of synchronizing persistent stores.
rawIndexableTextContent: The central class of Ensembles, representing a set of synchronizing persistent stores.   Overview An ensemble monitors a single Core Data persistent store, records local saves as events, and exchanges these events with peers via a CloudFileSystem backend to reach eventual consistency. You typically create one CoreDataEnsemble for each persistent store you need to sync. Ensemble instances with the same ensembleIdentifier on different devices sync their persistent stores together. Lifecycle Create the ensemble with your store URL, model URL, and a cloud backend. Call attachPersistentStore(seedPolicy:) to register with the cloud and import existing store data. Avoid saving to the persistent store during attachment. Call sync(options:) periodically to exchange data with peers. Call detachPersistentStore() to stop syncing and remove local sync data. All async operations are serialized internally — only one attach, detach, or sync runs at a time.

title: Migrating from Ensembles 2
summary: Transition an existing Ensembles 2 (Objective-C) app to Ensembles 3.
rawIndexableTextContent: Transition an existing Ensembles 2 (Objective-C) app to Ensembles 3. Overview Ensembles 3 is fully backward compatible with Ensembles 2 cloud data. You can migrate without a data reset — existing cloud files, event stores, and CloudKit records are all readable by E3 out of the box. What’s Backward Compatible Cloud files — The directory structure and file formats in the cloud are identical. E3 reads all E2 cloud data. Event store — The Core Data model for the local event database (CDEEventStoreModel) is unchanged. JSON format — Property change values use the same JSON serialization. CloudKit records — Record types (CDEFileSystemNode, CDEItem, CDEDataFile) and field names are preserved. Encryption — E3’s .legacy encryption format (RNCryptor v3) matches E2’s CDEEncryptedCloudFileSystem. Reading E2 data works regardless of compatibility mode — compatibility mode only restricts what E3 exports. Step-by-Step Migration 1. Swap the dependency Replace the Ensembles 2 CocoaPod or framework with the Ensembles 3 Swift package:  Or add it via Xcode’s Add Package Dependencies menu. 2. Set compatibility mode If some users may still be running the E2 version of your app, set CompatibilityMode.ensembles2Compatible so E3 restricts its exports to formats E2 peers can parse:  3. Update API names Ensembles 3 uses different class and method names: Ensembles 2 (Objective-C) Ensembles 3 (Swift) CDEPersistentStoreEnsemble CoreDataEnsemble CDEPersistentStoreEnsembleDelegate CoreDataEnsembleDelegate leechPersistentStore(...) CoreDataEnsemble/attachPersistentStore() deleechPersistentStore(...) detachPersistentStore() mergeWithCompletion: sync(options:) CDECloudFileSystem CloudFileSystem CDEEncryptedCloudFileSystem EncryptedCloudFileSystem CDECloudKitFileSystem CloudKitFileSystem  4. Test thoroughly Verify your app can read existing cloud data from E2 peers. Confirm that E2 peers can still read data exported by your E3 build (while in .ensembles2Compatible mode). Test attachment, sync, and detach cycles. 5. Switch to full E3 mode Once all users have upgraded to the E3 version of your app, change the compatibility mode to unlock future E3-only features:  What `ensembles2Compatible` Restricts Currently, CompatibilityMode.ensembles2Compatible applies one restriction: Model hashes: Forces compressModelHashes to false, so model version hashes use the full XML property list format that E2 peers can parse. No other export behavior is changed. As E3 evolves, additional restrictions may be added to this mode to maintain E2 compatibility.

title: Custom Cloud Backends
summary: Implement the CloudFileSystem protocol to sync via any storage service.
rawIndexableTextContent: Implement the CloudFileSystem protocol to sync via any storage service. Overview Ensembles exchanges sync data through a CloudFileSystem — a protocol that abstracts file storage behind a simple path-based API. Any service that can store and retrieve files at paths can serve as a backend: cloud storage (S3, Google Cloud Storage), file servers (WebDAV, SFTP), databases, or even peer-to-peer connections with a local cache. The CloudFileSystem Protocol The core protocol has 8 requirements organized into four groups: Connection  isConnected — Return whether the backend is ready for operations. connect() — Establish a connection. Called before each attach or merge. fetchUserIdentity() — Return a token identifying the current user, or nil if identity tracking is not supported. Ensembles uses this to detect account changes (e.g., signing out of iCloud). File Operations  Paths are forward-slash separated, rooted at /. The ensemble creates a directory structure like /MyEnsemble/events/, /MyEnsemble/baselines/, etc. Directory listings should return CloudFile or CloudDirectory items with their path and name populated. Only list immediate children — do not recurse. File Transfer  Upload copies a local file to the remote path. Download copies a remote file to the local path. Both paths are absolute on their respective systems (local paths are filesystem paths; remote paths are within the cloud file system’s namespace). A Minimal Example Here is a skeleton implementation to illustrate the structure:  Reference Implementations Study the built-in backends as starting points: MemoryCloudFileSystem (in EnsemblesMemory) — The simplest implementation. An actor that stores files in a dictionary. Good for understanding the protocol’s expectations. LocalCloudFileSystem (in EnsemblesLocalFile) — Uses FileManager to store files in a local directory. Shows how to map the protocol onto a real file system. CloudKitFileSystem (in EnsemblesCloudKit) — A production backend using CloudKit. Shows how to implement batch operations and setup hooks. PCloudCloudFileSystem (in EnsemblesPCloud) — A URLSession-based REST client with no SDK dependency. A good model for building backends against any REST API. BoxCloudFileSystem (in EnsemblesBox) — Wraps the Box Swift SDK. Demonstrates how to bridge an ID-based API (where files are identified by numeric IDs, not paths) onto the path-based CloudFileSystem protocol using folder-walking and caching. S3CloudFileSystem (in EnsemblesS3) — Wraps the AWS SDK for Swift. Shows how to simulate directory semantics on a flat key-value store using key prefixes and delimiters. Optional: Batch Operations If your backend can handle multiple files in a single request (reducing round trips), adopt CloudFileSystemBatchOperations:  The fileUploadMaximumBatchSize and fileDownloadMaximumBatchSize properties control how many files Ensembles passes per batch call. Default is 10 if you don’t override them. Optional: Setup Hooks For backends that need initialization, caching, or repair logic, adopt CloudFileSystemSetup:  Threading and Sendable Cloud file systems must conform to Sendable. If your implementation uses mutable state, you have several options: Use actor isolation (as MemoryCloudFileSystem does) Use @unchecked Sendable with explicit locking (as LocalCloudFileSystem does) Make the type stateless, delegating state to a thread-safe service All protocol methods are async throws, so you can freely call async APIs in your implementation.

title: Conflict Resolution
summary: Understand how Ensembles resolves conflicts and how to customize the process.
rawIndexableTextContent: Understand how Ensembles resolves conflicts and how to customize the process. Overview When multiple devices change the same data concurrently, conflicts arise. Ensembles resolves these automatically using causal revision tracking, and provides delegate hooks for cases that need custom logic. Automatic Resolution Every event carries a revision set that records which events from other peers have been seen. When events are replayed during a merge, the integrator determines the causal ordering: if one change “happened after” another (it had seen the other’s revision), that change wins. For truly concurrent changes to the same attribute, the most recent timestamp is used as a tiebreaker. This means that for most apps, conflicts are resolved transparently without any code on your part. The Reparation Context For cases where automatic resolution isn’t sufficient, the delegate provides a two-context pattern for inspecting and repairing merged data before it’s committed. Inspecting Changes Before the merge save, the ensemble calls:  The savingContext contains all the merged changes about to be committed. Use it to inspect what will happen:  Making Repairs Do not modify the savingContext directly. Instead, make corrections in the reparationContext:  Changes made in the reparation context are captured as new events, so they propagate to all peers on the next merge. The saving context and reparation context have a parent-child relationship. Do not nest perform or performAndWait calls between them — this will deadlock. Handling Save Failures If the merge save fails (e.g., due to a validation error), the ensemble calls:  Inspect the error to determine which objects caused the failure, make corrections in the reparationContext, and return true to reattempt the save. Return false to abort. Global Identifiers and Deduplication When the same logical object is created independently on multiple devices, automatic merging produces duplicates — two objects with different identifiers but the same meaning (e.g., two “Work” tags). To handle this, implement the global identifier delegate method:  When Ensembles encounters objects with matching global identifiers from different devices, it merges them into a single object rather than creating duplicates. Global identifiers must be immutable — once assigned, an object’s identifier must never change. If you need to change an identifier, delete the object and create a new one. Forced Detaches In exceptional circumstances, the ensemble may be forced to detach: The cloud identity changed (e.g., the user signed out of iCloud) The hardware identity changed unexpectedly Data corruption was detected (incomplete mandatory events) The device’s registration was removed from the cloud When this happens, the ensemble calls coreDataEnsemble(_:didDetachWithError:). You should update your UI to reflect the detached state and can attempt to re-attach to resume syncing.

title: Architecture
summary: Understand how Ensembles synchronizes Core Data stores using event sourcing.
rawIndexableTextContent: Understand how Ensembles synchronizes Core Data stores using event sourcing. Overview Ensembles uses an event-sourcing architecture. Every change to a Core Data persistent store is recorded as an event. Events are exchanged between devices through a pluggable cloud backend and replayed to bring each device’s store into a consistent state. Event Sourcing Model When the app saves to the monitored persistent store, a SaveMonitor captures the inserted, updated, and deleted objects and records them as a modification event in a separate event store database. Each event records the full set of property changes and is tagged with a revision that tracks causal ordering across peers. The event store is a separate Core Data stack from your app’s store — Ensembles never modifies your model or schema. Events are stored locally until they are exported to the cloud, and remote events are imported and kept locally until they are integrated. The Sync Flow A call to sync(options:) proceeds through several phases: 1. Preparation The ensemble connects to the cloud backend, verifies the device’s identity (cloud account and hardware), and checks that the device is still registered. If identity has changed (e.g., the user signed out of iCloud), the ensemble forces a detach. 2. Import New data files, baselines, and events are downloaded from the cloud and stored locally. File downloads can be batched for backends that support CloudFileSystemBatchOperations. 3. Baseline Consolidation Old events are compacted into a baseline snapshot. A baseline captures the full state of the store at a point in time, allowing events before that point to be discarded. This keeps the sync data from growing without bound. 4. Rebasing When the event history grows large, the ensemble can rebase — compressing the history relative to the latest baseline. Rebasing runs automatically when Ensembles estimates a significant space saving, or can be triggered with forceRebase. 5. Integration Remote events are replayed into the persistent store in three stages: Insert — New objects are created in the store. Update — Attributes and relationships are updated. When multiple peers have changed the same attribute, the value from the most recent event (by causal ordering) wins. Delete — Objects marked as deleted are removed. Before the integrated changes are saved, the delegate can inspect and repair them via coreDataEnsemble(_:shouldSaveMergedChangesIn:reparationContext:). 6. Export New local events, baselines, and data files are uploaded to the cloud. This phase can be suppressed with suppressCloudFileDeposition. 7. Cleanup Outdated remote files (events that have been superseded by baselines) are removed from the cloud. Key Components Component Role CoreDataEnsemble Main coordinator. Serializes all operations via an internal AsyncStream. EventStore Manages the local event database (a separate Core Data stack). CloudManager Orchestrates cloud file operations: snapshot, import, export, cleanup. EventIntegrator Replays remote events into the app’s persistent store. SaveMonitor Observes NSManagedObjectContextDidSave to capture local changes. RevisionManager Tracks causal ordering of events across distributed peers. BaselineConsolidator / Rebaser Consolidates event history into compact snapshots.  Cloud Backends All cloud backends implement the CloudFileSystem protocol. This is the primary extension point. See Custom Cloud Backends for a guide to adding your own. Two optional protocols extend the base: CloudFileSystemBatchOperations — Adds batch upload, download, and delete for backends that support them (reduces round trips). CloudFileSystemSetup — Adds one-time preparation, priming, and directory repair hooks. Model Versioning Each event is stamped with a model version hash (either a full entity-hash dictionary or a compressed MD5). During integration, RevisionManager checks every incoming event’s model version against the local managedObjectModels array — a list of all NSManagedObjectModel instances the app supports. When hashes match, the event is integrable and proceeds through the sync flow. When hashes don’t match, the event is flagged with EnsembleError.unknownModelVersion and excluded from integration. This prevents applying changes that reference entities or attributes the current app version doesn’t understand. Providing Model Versions For Core Data projects using .xcdatamodeld bundles, the URL-based convenience initializers on CoreDataEnsemble automatically load all model versions from the bundle via loadAllModelVersions(from:). For SwiftData projects, SwiftDataEnsemble builds in-memory models from @Model types or VersionedSchema entries. You can also pass managedObjectModels directly to the designated initializer when you have in-memory models from other sources. The `managedObjectModels` Array If managedObjectModels is nil, model version checking is disabled and all events are treated as integrable. This is appropriate for testing but not recommended in production. If the array is non-nil, it must contain all model versions that any peer in the sync group might produce. When a peer upgrades its schema and starts emitting events with a new model hash, other peers must also be updated to include that model version or they will reject those events. Handling Breaking Migrations Ensembles does not perform Core Data model migration itself — it only checks compatibility. If you add or rename entities or attributes, you must: Include both the old and new NSManagedObjectModel in the managedObjectModels array. Perform any necessary lightweight or custom migration on the app’s persistent store before attaching the ensemble. Ensure all peers in the sync group are updated to understand the new schema before they sync events from upgraded peers. Data Flow Diagram  Local saves flow right: captured by the event store, then exported to the cloud. Remote changes flow left: imported from the cloud into the event store, then integrated into the app’s store.

title: Getting Started
summary: Set up Ensembles to sync a Core Data persistent store across devices.
rawIndexableTextContent: Set up Ensembles to sync a Core Data persistent store across devices. Overview Ensembles synchronizes a Core Data SQLite store by recording local changes as events and exchanging them through a cloud backend. This guide walks through the essential steps, starting with the simplest approach using CoreDataEnsembleContainer. Add Ensembles to Your Project Add the Ensembles package to your Package.swift or via Xcode’s package dependencies. Import the core module and one cloud backend:  The Easy Way: EnsembleContainer CoreDataEnsembleContainer handles the most common setup: it creates the Core Data stack, sets up a delegate that auto-merges remote changes, and syncs automatically on save, on app activation, and on a timer.  The container automatically attaches to the cloud and begins syncing. It provides a main-queue viewContext for your UI and merges remote changes into it automatically. Use didSaveMergeChanges for additional processing, and didEncounterError or didForceDetach for error handling. Working with an Existing Stack If you already have an NSPersistentContainer or NSManagedObjectContext, pass it in:  Auto-Sync Configuration Control when syncing occurs with AutoSyncPolicy:  Set autoSyncPolicy to .manual and call sync() yourself for full control. Backward Compatibility If you’re transitioning from Ensembles 2 and some users may still be on E2, set the compatibility mode:  This restricts exports to formats that Ensembles 2 peers can parse. Switch to .ensembles3 (the default) once all users have upgraded. The Manual Way: CoreDataEnsemble For more control over the sync lifecycle, use CoreDataEnsemble directly. Create an Ensemble Initialize a CoreDataEnsemble with your persistent store URL, managed object model URL, and a cloud file system:  The ensembleIdentifier must be the same on all devices that should sync together. If you have multiple persistent stores, create one ensemble per store with a unique identifier. Attach to the Cloud Before syncing, the ensemble must attach — this registers the device in the cloud and imports existing store data into the event log:  Attachment is persistent: once attached, the ensemble stays attached across app launches until you explicitly detach. Check isAttached to determine the current state. Avoid saving to the persistent store while attaching. If a save is detected, attachment will fail with an error and you will need to retry. Seed Policies The SeedPolicy parameter controls how existing data in the store is handled: SeedPolicy.mergeAllData (default) — Imports local data so it can be merged with data from other devices. SeedPolicy.excludeLocalData — Ignores local data. Use this when you want to start fresh from cloud data, typically after deleting and recreating the store. Sync Changes Call sync(options:) periodically to sync — for example, when the app enters the foreground, on a timer, or in response to a push notification:  A sync downloads remote events, replays them into the local store, and uploads new local events. Sync errors are usually transient (incomplete downloads, save interruptions) — just retry later. Sync Options For advanced control, pass SyncOptions to customize which phases run:  Handle the Delegate Implement CoreDataEnsembleDelegate to respond to sync events. The most important method merges remote changes into your main context:  Other delegate methods let you: Inspect and repair merge data before it’s committed (shouldSaveMergedChangesIn:reparationContext:) Handle save failures during merging (didFailToSaveMergedChangesIn:error:reparationContext:) React to forced detaches caused by identity changes or data corruption (didDetachWithError:) Supply global identifiers for automatic deduplication (globalIdentifiersForManagedObjects:) See Conflict Resolution for more on conflict handling and the reparation context. Detach To stop syncing, detach from the cloud:  This removes local sync data (event logs and metadata). The persistent store itself is not affected. You can re-attach later to resume syncing. Process Pending Changes On iOS, the app may be suspended before Ensembles finishes recording a save. summary: Sync SwiftData models with Ensembles using SwiftDataEnsemble and SwiftDataEnsembleContainer.
title: SwiftData Integration
rawIndexableTextContent: Sync SwiftData models with Ensembles using SwiftDataEnsemble and SwiftDataEnsembleContainer. Overview The EnsemblesSwiftData module provides SwiftData-native APIs for Ensembles sync. Under the hood it converts SwiftData @Model types to Core Data models and delegates to CoreDataEnsemble, but you never need to touch Core Data directly. Quick Start with SwiftDataEnsembleContainer The easiest way to add sync is with SwiftDataEnsembleContainer. It creates a ModelContainer, sets up auto-sync, and handles the delegate:  The container auto-syncs on save, on app activation, and on a timer. Configure this with EnsembleContainerConfiguration:  Global Identifiers with `Syncable` When two devices independently create the “same” object (e.g., a tag with the same name), Ensembles needs a way to recognize they represent the same entity and merge them instead of duplicating. This is called deduplication and requires a global identifier. SwiftData models declare a global identifier by conforming to the Syncable protocol:  The globalIdentifierKey names a property whose value uniquely identifies the object across all devices. When two objects of the same entity have the same global identifier value, Ensembles merges them into one. Not all entities need global identifiers — only those where independent creation of “the same” object is likely (reference data, categories, user profiles, etc.). Entities that are always created explicitly by the user (notes, photos, messages) typically don’t need them. Advanced: SwiftDataEnsembleDelegate For cases where Syncable isn’t sufficient — such as compound identifiers or conditional logic — use SwiftDataEnsembleDelegate:  An explicit delegate takes priority over Syncable conformance. Using SwiftDataEnsemble Directly If you manage your own ModelContainer or need more control, use SwiftDataEnsemble directly:  Access the underlying CoreDataEnsemble via the .ensemble property for advanced operations:  Model Versioning with VersionedSchema When your SwiftData model evolves, supply all versions via a SchemaMigrationPlan:  Ensembles extracts each VersionedSchema and uses the models for version compatibility checking during sync. This ensures that events from older peers (using an earlier schema) are recognized and handled correctly. Persistent History Tracking SwiftDataEnsemble enables persistent history tracking by default via defaultPersistentStoreOptions. This allows SwiftData to detect changes that Ensembles merges into the store, so your @Query properties and ModelContext stay up to date automatically. If you provide custom persistentStoreOptions, make sure to include these keys:

Sync Core Data stores across devices using event sourcing. Overview Ensembles synchronizes Core Data persistent stores by recording local saves as events, exchanging them through a pluggable cloud backend, and replaying them on each peer to reach eventual consistency. An CoreDataEnsemble monitors a single persistent store. When the app saves, Ensembles captures the changes automatically. Calling sync(options:) downloads remote events, replays them into the local store, and uploads new local events. Conflicts are resolved through causal revision tracking, with delegate hooks for custom repair logic. Quick Start The simplest way to add sync is with CoreDataEnsembleContainer, which creates the Core Data stack, handles the delegate, and auto-syncs on save:  For more control, use CoreDataEnsemble directly:  Cloud Backends Ensembles ships with several cloud backends: CloudKitFileSystem — Syncs via iCloud using CloudKit. GoogleDriveCloudFileSystem — Syncs via Google Drive REST API v3. OneDriveCloudFileSystem — Syncs via Microsoft OneDrive (Graph API v1.0). PCloudCloudFileSystem — Syncs via pCloud, a Swiss provider with strong privacy guarantees. WebDAVCloudFileSystem — Syncs via any WebDAV server (Nextcloud, ownCloud, etc.). EncryptedCloudFileSystem — Wraps any backend with AES-256-GCM encryption. LocalCloudFileSystem — Uses a local directory, useful for testing or shared-folder sync. MemoryCloudFileSystem — An in-memory actor-based backend for unit testing. Any storage that can hold files at paths can serve as a backend. See Custom Cloud Backends for a guide to implementing your own. Backward Compatibility Ensembles 3 reads and writes Ensembles 2 cloud data and event store formats. Existing apps can migrate without a data reset. See Migrating from Ensembles 2 for a step-by-step guide.

rawIndexableTextContent: Removes the given element and all elements subsumed by it.  The element of the set to remove. Return Value The intersection of [member] and the set, if the intersection was nonempty; otherwise, nil. Discussion In the following example, the .priority shipping option is removed from the options option set. Attempting to remove the same shipping option a second time results in nil, because options no longer contains .priority as a member.  In the next example, the .express element is passed to remove(_:). Although .express is not a member of options, .express subsumes the remaining .secondDay element of the option set. Therefore, options is emptied and the intersection between .express and options is returned.
summary: Removes the given element and all elements subsumed by it.
title: remove(_:)
